defs: 3d pers. sing. pres. of Hide, contracted from hideth.

records.ety: [OE. hitten, hutten, of Scand. origin; cf. Dan. hitte to hit, find, Sw. & Icel. hitta.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Hit; p. pr. & vb. n. Hitting.]

defs: To reach with a stroke or blow; to strike or touch, usually with force; especially, to reach or touch (an object aimed at).
quotes: I think you have hit the mark.

defs: To reach or attain exactly; to meet according to the occasion; to perform successfully; to attain to; to accord with; to be conformable to; to suit.
quotes: Birds learning tunes, and their endeavors to hit the notes right.
There you hit him; . . . that argument never fails with him.
Whose saintly visage is too bright To hit the sense of human sight.

defs: To guess; to light upon or discover.
To take up, or replace by a piece belonging to the opposing player; -- said of a single unprotected piece on a point.
To meet or come in contact; to strike; to clash; -- followed by against or on.
quotes: If bodies be extension alone, how can they move and hit one against another?
Corpuscles, meeting with or hitting on those bodies, become conjoined with them.

defs: To meet or reach what was aimed at or desired; to succeed, -- often with implied chance, or luck.
quotes: And oft it hits Where hope is coldest and despair most fits.
And millions miss for one that hits.

defs: A striking against; the collision of one body against another; the stroke that touches anything.
quotes: So he the famed Cilician fencer praised, And, at each hit, with wonder seems amazed.

defs: A stroke of success in an enterprise, as by a fortunate chance; as, he made a hit;

A performance, as a musical recording, movie, or play, which achieved great popularity or acclaim; also used of books or objects of commerce which become big sellers; as, the new notebook computer was a big hit with business travellers.
quotes: What late he called a blessing, now was wit, And God's good providence, a lucky hit.

defs: A peculiarly apt expression or turn of thought; a phrase which hits the mark; as, a happy hit.
A game won at backgammon after the adversary has removed some of his men. It counts less than a gammon.
A striking of the ball; as, a safe hit; a foul hit; -- sometimes used specifically for a base hit.
An act of murder performed for hire, esp. by a professional assassin.
Having become very popular or acclaimed; -- said of entertainment performances; as, a hit song, a hit movie.
